title: The Money Wound of Being Told You Were Too Much
body:
The Money Wound of Being Told You Were Too Much

Here's the thing: you carry a deep money wound, and it's not about your spending habits. It's not about your budget. It's not even about that one bad investment you made years ago. No, this wound goes back further, deeper. It's the wound of being told, implicitly or explicitly, that you were just too much. Too loud. Too emotional. Too ambitious. Too sensitive. Too needy. Too expensive. Too... you. And somewhere along the line, your brilliant, expansive self learned to shrink, to dim its light, to apologize for its very existence, especially when it came to money. You feel that?

This isn't some fluffy self-help concept. This is a real, visceral pain that shapes your financial reality today. What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? It reflects every insecurity, every fear, every limiting belief you hold about your worthiness, your capacity, your right to take up space and thrive. And when you were told you were "too much," it directly attacked your sense of inherent worth. It told you your natural state was somehow wrong, an imposition, a burden. This narrative, insidious and persistent, then gets intertwined with your financial story. You learn to believe that if you want too much, ask for too much, earn too much, or even simply have too much, you'll be rejected, shamed, or abandoned. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It's etched into your nervous system, a constant hum of inadequacy.

The Echoes of "Too Much" in Your Wallet

Think about it. When you were told you were "too much," what did that feel like? A tightening in your chest? A flush of shame? A desperate urge to disappear? That same physiological response, that same emotional memory, gets triggered when you're faced with financial decisions that push against those old boundaries. You might find yourself:

  • Underearning, despite your skills and experience. You unconsciously cap your income because more money feels like "too much" for you to handle, too much attention, too much responsibility, too much visibility.
  • Struggling to ask for raises or negotiate better rates. The thought of advocating for your worth feels audacious, arrogant, "too much."
  • Having trouble receiving. Compliments, gifts, help - and especially money - can feel uncomfortable, like you don't deserve them, like you're taking "too much."
  • Overgiving, constantly pouring yourself out for others, financially and otherwise, often to your own detriment. This is a desperate attempt to prove you're "enough" by being indispensable, a way to compensate for feeling "too much."
  • Sabotaging your financial success. Just when things are going well, you might make a "mistake" that brings you back down to a more "acceptable" level, a level where you're not "too much."
  • Feeling guilt or shame around having money, even if you've worked hard for it. You might hide your success, downplay your achievements, or feel compelled to apologize for your abundance.
  • Avoiding financial conversations or planning altogether. It's easier to stay small and invisible than to confront the bigness of your financial potential, which feels "too much."

Does that land? These aren't just quirks of personality. These are intelligent, protective mechanisms developed by your younger self to survive a world that told you your natural expression was problematic. Your nervous system learned to associate expansion with danger, and contraction with safety. And money, being a primary vehicle for expansion and taking up space in the world, becomes a battleground for these old wounds.

Where Did This Story Begin?

Brad Klontz, a pioneer in financial psychology, talks extensively about "money scripts" - unconscious beliefs about money that we absorb from childhood. When you were told you were "too much," it wasn't just a casual comment. It was a money script being written into your subconscious. Perhaps your parents struggled financially and your desires felt like a burden. "We can't afford that, you're asking for too much." Perhaps you were a highly sensitive child, and your big emotions were met with impatience or dismissal. "Stop being so dramatic, you're too much." Maybe you came from a family where modesty was prized, and ambition was seen as vulgar. "Don't get too big for your britches."

These messages, often delivered with good intentions (or out of their own unhealed wounds), shaped your understanding of self-worth and its connection to resources. Geneen Roth, in her powerful work on emotional eating and money, often points out how our relationship with food mirrors our relationship with money. Both are about sustenance, worthiness, and control. When you restrict yourself from food, or binge on it, it's often a reflection of deeper feelings of not being "enough" or being "too much." The same applies to money. You might restrict your earning potential, or you might overspend as a way to temporarily feel "enough," only to crash back into feelings of "too much" debt or shame.

The body remembers. Bessel van der Kolk's The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) isn't just about big T traumas. It's about the cumulative effect of seemingly small messages that wire our nervous system for survival. Your body holds the memory of being shut down, of being told to quiet your desires, of being made to feel wrong for your natural exuberance. And that memory directly impacts your capacity to hold and generate wealth.

Reclaiming Your "Too Much-ness"

So, how do you begin to heal this? It starts with awareness. You're reading this, so you've already taken the first, most crucial step. You're acknowledging the wound. Now, you get to sit with it. Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. Not a financial number, but the number of times you've allowed yourself to shrink, to dim, to play small because of that old story.

Healing isn't about changing who you are. It's about remembering who you were before the world told you to be less. It's about reclaiming your inherent "too much-ness" - your passion, your sensitivity, your ambition, your unique way of being - and realizing that these are not flaws, but superpowers. Lynne Twist, in The Soul of Money (paid link), talks about sufficiency. It's not about having "enough" in a material sense, but about recognizing that you ARE enough. You are inherently sufficient. And your "too much-ness" is simply your full expression of that sufficiency.

Here's how to start:

1. Witness the Triggers

Pay attention to when the "too much" feeling arises around money. Is it when you're about to ask for a raise? When you're looking at a big purchase? When you see someone else's financial success? Notice the physical sensations in your body. The tightening, the shame, the urge to retreat. Don't judge it. Just witness it. This is your body telling you an old story is playing out.

2. Challenge the Narrative

When that voice whispers "you're too much," ask yourself: "Is that really true? Or is that an old echo?" Who told you that? What were their unhealed wounds? You are not responsible for carrying their limitations. Sam Harris, in his discussions on consciousness, often emphasizes the importance of observing our thoughts rather than being consumed by them. Your thoughts are not always facts. They're often just old recordings.

3. Practice Receiving

This is huge. Start small. Let someone hold the door for you without rushing to return the favor. Accept a compliment without deflecting it. Allow a friend to treat you to coffee. Then, gradually, extend this to money. Practice receiving payments for your work with gratitude, not guilt. See if you can read Jen Sincero's You Are a Badass at Making Money (paid link) and actually apply the lessons without feeling like you're being "too much." The act of receiving, without apology or immediate reciprocation, directly challenges the "too much" wound. It says, "I am worthy of this. I am enough."

4. Embrace Your Desires

What do you truly want? Not what you think you *should* want, or what's "realistic," but what truly lights you up? Write it down. Don't censor yourself. These desires are not "too much." They are the compass to your most expansive life. Your "too much-ness" is your unique blueprint for contribution and joy. You're not being selfish by wanting. You're being authentic. Know what I mean?

5. Set Boundaries

Often, the "too much" wound makes us terrible at boundaries. We let others overstep, take advantage, or drain our resources because saying "no" feels like being "too much" or selfish. Learning to say "no" is an act of self-reclamation. It's a clear statement that your time, energy, and resources are valuable, and you get to decide how they're used. This is not being "too much." This is being respectful of yourself.

This isn't a quick fix. This is deep, soul-level work. It requires patience, compassion, and a fierce commitment to your own liberation. But the payoff? A life where you're not just surviving, but truly thriving, financially and otherwise. A life where your "too much-ness" is celebrated, not suppressed. A life where you are unapologetically, magnificently YOU.

FAQ

What if I don't remember being explicitly told I was "too much"?

You don't need a specific memory. Often, these messages are subtle, absorbed through observation, tone of voice, or the general atmosphere of your upbringing. Perhaps you felt you had to be quiet to be loved, or small to be safe. The feeling itself - the contraction, the shame, the urge to hide your desires - is the evidence. Trust your inner experience. The body remembers, even if the conscious mind doesn't have a clear narrative.

Is this just an excuse for my financial problems?

Absolutely not. This is an explanation, not an excuse. Understanding the root cause of a problem is the first step towards solving it. Blaming yourself for financial struggles without understanding the underlying emotional and psychological patterns is like trying to fix a leaky pipe by constantly mopping up the floor. You need to go to the source. This work isn't about avoiding responsibility; it's about taking radical responsibility for your healing so you can build a truly solid financial foundation.

How long does this healing process take?

Healing is not linear, and it's not a destination. It's an ongoing journey of self-awareness and conscious choice. You'll have breakthroughs, and you'll have moments where old patterns resurface. The key is to keep showing up for yourself, keep practicing the tools, and keep extending compassion. Some shifts can happen quickly, while others unfold over years. Be patient. Be persistent. Every conscious choice you make to honor your worth chips away at that old wound.

Can I really become financially abundant if I carry this wound?

Yes, absolutely. In fact, healing this wound is often the missing piece for those who struggle with financial abundance. When you release the belief that you are "too much," you naturally open yourself up to receiving more, earning more, and holding more. You stop unconsciously sabotaging your success. Your capacity expands. It's not about changing the external world; it's about changing your internal relationship to worthiness and expansion. The external results then follow. It's about aligning your inner world with the abundance you desire in your outer world. You are already abundant in your essence; this work is about allowing that truth to manifest financially.

This isn't about blaming your past. It's about understanding it, so you can consciously choose a different future. A future where your "too much-ness" is your greatest asset. A future where you're not just earning a living, but living a life of expansive, unapologetic worth. So, today, right now, take one small action. Write down one desire you've been suppressing because it felt "too much." Just write it down. No judgment. No plan. Just acknowledge it. That's where the healing begins.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Heal your money wound from being told you were "too much." Understand how past messages impact your finances and learn to redefine your worth.
author: Kalesh
